speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this is tape 5 of the interview with John Horak. It's the 18th of June, 1995, and we are in Sydney, Australia. OK, camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you returned to Bratislava after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to talk a little bit about your life after the war in Europe.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was there for you in Bratislava when you returned?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very dramatic experience and a very sad one, because everything was rubbed out, actually, from our beautiful home, or flat. My father didn't come back. Unfortunately, we knew it. And one of my brothers, the second oldest from me, he was the only one who was taken to Auschwitz and from Auschwitz in various other camps. And he didn't return when almost everybody was home from Jewish population in our home town. He came about three months later. He was, uh, liberated in Germany in one of-- I forgot even the camp. And when the Russians liberated them, whoever was able to walk, they-- they, uh, took everything from Germany-- the Russians-- and sent it back to Russia, and rightly so. So they-- they took about 20 or 30 cows that was in the country town, collected about half a dozen of the poor, surviving Jews who were able still to stand on their feet, and they said, you will drove these cows to Poland and maybe to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he did that for about two months, because they were so scared, you know? They didn't have a clue who lives, who doesn't live. And they were driving the cows, which were from different villages, on the way. The Russians went also with them. But they took more and more, and they needed the drivers for the-- for the animals.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was one of the unlucky ones who was caught. And because he was suffering so much during his time in the concentration camp, he didn't dare-- he didn't have the strength to run away. Many of them run away. They said, it doesn't matter. But they were mostly Polish youngsters or Jews, who-- because they entered Poland. And they were at home. My brother knew he has to walk other 1,000 kilometers to reach Bratislava, which was impossible. But in the end, he got somehow the courage, and he ran away with some other people and was walking and walking. And therefore, he came almost three months after we were already settled. Now, when I came home, of course we had to start a new life. And, uh, we were lucky insofar as we found at least the house standing. Furniture and all the other things were stolen. But also, we found some goods because the trustee was still active to the last days of the war-- the non-Jewish trustee which we had. And we prepared over the year before, or even two years before-- we had, under the huge shop premises which we had, we had a subterrain, which is like a basement. And in the basement, I remember, when I was still free, that we put very valuable things which were important. Part of our stock we always were hiding there. And, uh, it was well hidden. So the soldiers, which were robbing the store, the retreating Germans and the coming Russian, they all started to rob any shop, which were still full of merchandise. They just walked in and robbed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we lost quite a lot. Matter of fact, the Russian army came there one day with huge trucks and loaded everything which they could take away from our big shop. And when my brother came and asked, what are you doing? You are Russian army. You are our liberator. They said, don't you worry-- when the trucks were loaded up to the rim. And my brother said, but, uh, have you got some papers? So he took a little notebook and wrote, in Russian, and handed it. Here, you have the paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: After they left, my brother had it translated, and it was written there in such sense, and, out of gratitude of being liberated by the victorious Red Army, Horak brothers-- because we were already under Horak brothers, carrying on-- gave these goods as a reward to the Red Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you know, the whole lot was illegal. Maybe that fellow who pinched it maybe sold it on the black market. So they were times, about for half a year, which were still unsettled. But you were running your business again?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were running our business. Because of our previous activity, we were the first, like named by the, uh, after-war government, like distributors. Because there was a shortage of everything. And if the-- the manufacturers didn't supply goods-- only by government order. And we had a big store before, so they said, all right, you will be supplying the people with shirts, ladies jumpers, and things like that, of clothing, mainly-- but only on coupons, because the public got coupons.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were trusted people, and we had that. So we were already busy. And I settled immediately with my older brother, who married, as I mentioned before, Mimi, which was my sister-in-law. They-- they had the wedding, and he married her.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was already married, was saved by some very good people, who saved them in a country town. They were hiding. My brother-in-law, when he was taken to the railway station-- because they-- in the end of '44, beginning already-- yes, end of '44 when the transport were just herded together, it was less organized. Whichever Jew was caught on the street or somewhere was just taken, put in the-- in the railway yard and in the, uh, cattle wagons.

speaker: interviewer
text: My sister had so much-- uh, my married sister to my brother-in-law-- had so much courage and fantasy that she dressed up in a fur coat, elegant with her hat, and she said, I am not Jewish. What are you taking me for? Because her husband was taken somewhere else, and she was taken from home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she insisted on her story to such extent, she was playing it, then the German soldiers, who were already guarding on this railway station, all right, go to hell. Don't be here. Because he was convinced she was not Jewish because they took sometimes non-Jewish people by accident.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she saved herself. She didn't know where to go. She was hiding in the cemetery for two nights. And from there, she was taken to country. And luckily, the husband jumped out from the going train, because he knew then the wife was safe. He saw her, how she walked out. And they were saved, and they were hiding in a village. And where did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, uh, met my wife because I knew the family. We come from the same town in Bratislava. I knew the family. I knew their sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we knew each other, not-- because she's a little bit younger than me. So when she was 10, I was already 16. So there wasn't, uh, any girlfriend idea behind it, but I knew the family. They were very prominent Jews, very rich men. So we knew each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I met my wife when she came home from-- from concentration camp and was recovering, was living with a distant uncle-- she told her story, too-- and the other old people, and she was preparing a-- a trip to Israel. Because after the war, there were a lot of orphans and a lot of young people who were left by themselves. So the Jewish community got organized very quickly and arranged transport of young children and young people to go to Israel, like Aliyah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But many of the girls who were in her age-- she was, uh, 17? Yes. She could be 18 at that time. They were arranged false marriages, because many of the Israeli boys who were in the-- there was a Czechoslovakian brigade in English army, and many of the Jewish, uh, boys who were serving in the English brigade of Czechoslovakian background-- they were Jewish boys-- and they came home to Bratislava to visit or to see who survived of such families-- very few.

speaker: interviewee
text: But these boys were still dressed in the battle dress of English army, got little bit of money, and they came to Bratislava. And this Jewish organization, the Aliyah organization, asked each one of these boys, look, make a-- a pretended marriage. Marry of some girls, because we can send them legally to Israel. Because the transport went illegally, you know, the exodus story and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you come to marry her?

speaker: interviewee
text: So all of these returned soldiers-- we were the best-stocked shop in town. So they came. Usually they were-- they were on a holiday, one week, sometimes two weeks. And before the holiday finished, they had a lot of local money, which was paper money for them, so they went in shop to shop and bought whatever they could. And we were very well-stocked with, uh, menswear. So they usually come to us and bought half a dozen shirts and socks and everything. And one-- and I was in the shop, like being the-- the junior owner. And I overheard one of the boys, who was very outspoken, he said, I'm going to marry a girl tomorrow. And they didn't make any secrets. You know, I am marrying a girl, who I take to Israel, and she can go on my passport as a wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was making jokes. And, well, I have a good time on the boat. Because they went by boat. And I couldn't hear it long enough, so I said, and who is this girl that you are talking? She is a beauty. And he told the name of my wife. I said, you are going to marry her? He said, yeah, she has nobody, and she applied. And she's on my list, and I was paired with her. I-- I'm looking forward. I said, yes, you do? And I made up my mind and my heart he is not going to have a good time. So I went to the auntie, who was where she was living, and I proposed to her. It took a little bit longer, but this is the short version. And of course, because she was really all by herself, so she didn't want to-- she was very much inclined to go to Israel because they were very religious and more Zionistic-inclined. We were also religious, but not to the extremes, you know? So did the two of you marry in Bratislava?

speaker: interviewee
text: I married in Bratislava in 1947. And I had to apply for a military and police-- not escort-- guard. Because we were occupied by Russian army. And you have no idea how many women and girls were raped, Jewish or not Jewish. It didn't matter. And I was very-- I had the connections still to the police through my brother, so when we had-- and we arranged the wedding in the only leading hotel, which was the Carlton Savoy in Bratislava. And we had the wedding ceremony. And I knew when there was somewhere a wedding ceremony, the Russians would flock there like-- like the-- the ants on the honey.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I bribed the police chief, and he gave me two policemen to stand at the door and drive them away. Mind you, they had such powers, and they could have thrown out the two policemen. But they didn't dare because it was the number one-- only high officer were staying in the hotel. So we had our wedding in '47, and I-- I gave her one condition, which I mentioned to everyone. I said, only you can marry me under one condition. She said, what is the condition? She would have married any condition. I said, we will marry, and we will go away. And she said, and where we will go? I said, I don't know. So you know, it was foolish. Because I was decided then, in the last, uh year-- that was in September we left the country-- I didn't want to stay there. For me, the whole country was like a huge cemetery.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, who married the non-Jewish girl, he was happy with her. My other brother had to recover. He married, uh, another Jewish girl, my second brother. And they didn't want to go, because our firm was well established, and they made good money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was the only one. I said, I leave everything here. You look after my part, and I go overseas. And I didn't know where to. Because when I asked for the passport to immigrate, I couldn't get one. Immigration to America, I got the affidavit. In '47 I got the affidavit, in beginning of '47. And when I went for the passport, they said, no. Where-- where you want to go? I had to go first to the consulate in Prague to get the-- the visa. If I get the passport, if I get the visa, and I had to produce a-- a ticket for the-- for the ship.

speaker: interviewee
text: The consulate in Prague said, no, you can't go. I showed him my-- my permit for me. It was a piece of gold. He said, no, you can't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, why? Because you are a liberated country-- Czechoslovakia-- you don't need to go. You have a quota of seven years. So I was absolutely, uh, like punched in the nose. So I came home, and I said, I still want to go under any circumstances. And I worked on it on a legal way, very, very carefully. It's a long story. I couldn't tell you.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were able to leave the country on a-- on a visiting passport for two weeks, passport which was only one month. And two-weeks' visa, we got to go to Hungary because there was a-- a commercial exhibition. It's very famous, even now. Every year is one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- on that pretense, I got the crossing of the border only as a local crossing. You could have crossed to Hungary, to Poland. All the countries which were occupied by Russia, they were safe. But when you wanted to go from Hungary to Austria, which was occupied by American and English and Russians, then you needed a proper passport. So where-- so how did you end up in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I-- we crossed the border, and Olga had an uncle who was, during the war-- in Canada-- a very wealthy man. And he was the very first overseas visitor, very first her relative and visitor from Canada. And he knew-- I was introduced. I was like the boyfriend who was going to marry Olga.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when he spoke to me, I said, look, we don't want to leave here. He said, yes, yes, you should go. Uh, you know, some people, especially the very people with a lot of money, they don't believe everything and what hardship you have, unless it is affecting them. So he said to me, look, uh, try to cross the country. Go to Switzerland, like if it would be crossing the harbor bridge. And when you will be in Switzerland, you ring me or send me a telegram. I will help you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was all. He was there, I think, couple of days. He took Olga to-- with his-- he had a brother there. The brother was taken to Canada. So he took them to Prague to say farewell to him. Olga was invited. And I was just like a complete outsider. I was already married to Olga, and she is the cousin of this uncle. And he said, no, I can't do anything. I will help you if you will be able to go to a neutral country, which was Switzerland. Switzerland is all right. You just get there. You know, for the Americans, or for any foreigner to say, like, go from here to New Zealand, it's easier than to go there after the war. So I was starting to work on that, and I must go. And to Switzerland was not easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So through my connection and money, I got, uh, the-- the passport for one month. So I went pro forma to Hungary, not through a main crossing. You know, there are little crossings of the border. So I selected specially a side border, where there were one or two people maybe the whole day going across.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went there, because for safety reasons, because we were very well known in Bratislava. If somebody would have recognized me and said, Horak is going to-- to Hungary, it was no danger. But to Switzerland, impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you go to Switzerland via Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Via Hungary. I-- we crossed the border to Austria. From Hungary to Austria, it was not so bad because I didn't need a visa to Austria. They were still without visa. We came to Vienna, and her-- she had a cousin, a distant cousin, living in Vienna. So we visited the distant cousin, but she was living in Russian zone. You know, there was Russian zone, English zone, and French zone. And whoever was living in Russian zone was always insecure because the Russians had such powers, and they could arrest you or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So her cousin-- when we came there, we were living there one day, and she said, look, it's no good for you. I-- I advise you, go to Switzerland. How can I go to Switzerland? So they-- they arranged-- no, I got-- I arranged a visa to Switzerland for one-- for two weeks, like a visitor's visa, also with money. And I got a stamp to have only limited-- fortnight visa to go to Switzerland. Then we bought the ticket on the train, and we went by train through to Zurich. So-- but I want to talk now about how you got to Australia. Why did you choose Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't choose. That is coincidence. I wanted to go to America or to Canada. And we were staying in Switzerland for over a year. And I was pestered by the Fremden Polizei, by the police of-- for foreigners. Every second month, you have to go, you have to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, where can I go? I have nowhere to go. I didn't have a visa anywhere. So I-- I told them the true stories, and I am waiting to get a visa to America or to Canada for-- by any means. If I succeed, it will take some time. So they were pushing me and pushing me every two months. I have to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't go to Canada or America, so, out of desperation, Olga had a cousin who came, uh, about half a year before to Melbourne. She knew the address, and Olga wrote him a letter with little tears on the paper, please help us. Because we were already desperate. We didn't know where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: The police threatened me, actually, in the last months, said if I don't make up my mind to leave, uh, Switzerland, I will be put on the border of either Austria or Germany, like a threat. I said, you just do that. From here, I go to which was the leading paper, and what you just told me-- Mr. Fry was his name, the inspector-- I said, I am going now to the newspaper and I will tell them the story as in how you treat the refugees. You want to put me on border of Germany, where I am running away? I don't want to see them.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, look, that is not our matter. You are a political uh, [NON-ENGLISH]. That is a political refugee. That is your worry, not ours.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the cousin sent for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cousin sent us a permit, and with the permit, we applied. And it took, I think, about a month till we-- we were approved by the English console in Zurich and all the necessary paper. And we came here by boat-- 32 days. 30 days of these 32 days, Olga was sick with sea sickness in bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you find when you came to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: A complete different world.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew the basic things about Australia. I knew the head capital towns, what they do, but I didn't realize how huge it is because it's a continent. Yes. The people were very friendly. I couldn't speak a word of English. Olga luckily spoke a little bit of English because as a child, her mother was very clever. She learned French and English. So she was my translator. But it was very, very tough. Luckily, we had a little bit of money. You know, without money, you are lost. But I was always-- not clever, but cautious. And I had some money when I went to Switzerland. Otherwise the Swiss people wouldn't be so tolerant. And I had still money left, little bit, when I came here, when I landed here in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody ever want to know about your experiences during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only later. Yeah. No. Maybe-- Who? Wanted to know? Not from the Australian people. I didn't even tell them, because they couldn't believe it. From other survivors who came, maybe from Austria. When we came here in '49, there were only a handful of German Jews. They were the very first who came here, '37, '38. There were Austrian Jews. And they were starting the influx of the migrants, mainly Czechoslovakia or Slovakia, and they settled in Melbourne. And then later on in '56, the Hungarians came. But the Hungarians were completely different. They didn't come out of necessity. They came only when the revolution started. Hungarians were living as free citizens till October '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we might stop it there and bring your family in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it is not finished properly, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, stay where you are. Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. Oh-- what does this mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop at 30.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, let's stop at 30.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll be full. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here we go.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Horak, you've got your family now with you. Can you introduce them to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, with pleasure. My oldest daughter Evelyn is standing behind me, my other daughter Suzi, and in the middle, a granddaughter, Kirsti, and Anthony, my grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh!

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm nobody. I'm forgotten. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you started with Evelyn, so I had to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, my dear wife, Olga. She's still very attractive lady, as you can see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And very, very up-to-date, efficient. No, I-- I-- really, I'm not talking in a joking way. She is the pillar of our family. You can ask my daughters and my grandchildren. And we-- what we have today, I can thank mainly to her. I may be the hard-working father, but she is a very creative mother. She started with me a business, which was very rewarding, and thanks to her ability, which I never knew she had such, uh, qualities, she is a designer, a fashion designer. And not only that, but she created every style which we produced. I was a manufacturer for 22 years in ladies fashion, unfortunately. [LAUGHS] And with her knowledge and her ability-- I am a textile expert, so I knew how to put a factory together and how to produce. But the finer points of designing and creating something which was-- she created, I think, about eight styles. We made blouses, mainly. She made eight samples. Each one was a seller. Well, that goes back in 1949 and 1950 and so on. But thanks to her ability to do that, maybe my life would have turned out different way if she wouldn't have had these qualities. So all my credit goes to you, my dear.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you've both--

speaker: interviewee
text: I never heard this much of a compliment over the 48 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] It's on video, as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks to that video.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, um-- so you've raised a beautiful family in Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope so.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think that your experiences during the war have impacted on your life and on the way you raised your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. I have maybe quite a special qualities of valuing things. Like my dear wife, she never throws anything out in her kitchen, except, uh, orange peels.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But everything else has to be utilized. Because we value-- and that is not for laughing-- we value the-- the quality of life, not as such that we can afford to live in a nice house, but our influence goes back to the hard times. Because we were quite well-- coming back from our families back home-- we were brought up in a very affluent family life. And we had to create it here almost from nothing. So if you can see, then it took a lot of work, and hard work. And we both together were always fully aware that we have to do it for the sake of our children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I maybe ask the daughters if you can tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how your parents' experiences were taught to you and what you knew about what they'd been through.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think their experience has made us strive for a very, uh, high education, um, so that we got what they were deprived of. And I think that was, you know, something that was really stressed through our childhood. And we both are high achievers. We've both got tertiary qualifications from universities. Um--

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were very high expectations. I guess we had to fulfill things--

speaker: interviewee
text: Perform.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that they-- that they were denied, educationally. Um, so it-- it was never really a matter of choice. The expectation was there that you have to. You have to achieve. You have to go to university. And you have to have a career to better yourself and not to have to endure the hardship, to make up for the sort of hardship and loss that our parents had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Mr. Horak, if I can just ask you one last question.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you could-- I mean, you've sat here and told me your story for the last two and a half hours. If you could--

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I?

speaker: interviewer
text: If you wanted to give a message to, I guess, my generation, what would it be?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be-- to be honest with yourself and with your fellow man. Because I found, through the very hard times, there was always somebody-- not necessarily a Jewish person-- if I was honest and I showed it in my-- not only behavior, but every act of my person, and they knew that I am a honest human being, and they helped me, sometimes unknowingly. So I always emphasize, even to my children, that they have to be correct and they have to behave towards other people also very correctly. And that is, in my opinion, very important also at fighting against anti-Semitism. Because we are accused of many things which are not true. So we have to create a atmosphere where we really are good people. And this I am trying to achieve all my life. And I did, I suppose. And that I try to impress my own children and grandchildren, who luckily didn't experience this thing, and I hope they never will. But we should be maybe a shining example what you can achieve if you are honest and if you work hard. Then the end result is a plus for everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I think also, both of their experiences have taught us to be proud of our heritage, not to be in  denial of anything and, in fact, to defend any form of discrimination.

speaker: interviewee
text: Prejudice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think that's very important so that that sort of disaster never happens again.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We might leave it there. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my late father, Samuel Hirschmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken-- I'm not sure of exact date, but it could be taken 1912 or 19-- maybe '14, just before the first World War because he was a military-- active military service in the First World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo could have been taken in Bratislava, most probably, or in our nearest, uh, bigger town, Trencin.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: It looks similar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo is again my late father. Uh, and the photo was taken in 1938 in a thermal place, in a holiday place, where we still went up to the year 1939. This particular photo was taken in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Samuel Hirschmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Hang on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now. Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this photo? From the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo, it's a very joyous occasion. It was on the bar mitzvah of my grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, uh, if I may describe from the left side, that is my daughter, Susan in the first row. Next to her, the little boy is Jonathan, her son. Then is my dear wife, Olga Horak. Then is the bar mitzvah boy, Anthony Levin, myself, then my other granddaughter, Kirsti, her mother Evelyn, and far right is my sister, Edith Volny.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about next to Suzi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to Suzi is the husband, Victor Berk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one was taken about three months ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is what date?

speaker: interviewee
text: February.

speaker: interviewee
text: In February--

speaker: interviewee
text: '95. And that was in--

speaker: interviewer
text: In February '95.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And that was taken in, uh--
